coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode sensing platform coupling two-signal ratiometric and colorimetric methods for detecting Au3+ based on surface state-regulated carbon nanodot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Hong Yu
Sun, Zhe
Deng, Cui Cui
Wang, Bin Jie
Dong, Xue Zhen
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: The considerable risk posed by Au3+ residues to the environment and human health has sparked interest in researching Au3+ monitoring techniques. The detection results in the usual ratio mode are more reliable. In this work, we develop a dual-mode strategy based on reducing carbon dots coupling with two-signal ratiometric and colorimetric methods for high-sensitivity, good-selectivity, and wide-range detection of Au3+. Cyan carbon dots (C-CDs) were synthesized by a simple and efficient one-step hydrothermal method. The C-CDs with rich amino group used m-phenylenediamine as carbon source, which made it have the potential as a reducing agent. After the addition of Au3+, Au3+ was reduced to Au0, generating stable gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The fluorescence signal (F 490) of C-CDs decreased. At the same time, the large size of AuNPs enhances the second-order scattering signal (S 770) and produces the UV–visible absorption peak of AuNPs. Therefore, the dual-mode sensing strategy combining S 770/F 490 ratiometric and colorimetric detection of Au3+ is realized with high accuracy and sensitivity. Au3+ was determined in real samples and a good recovery was obtained. The dual-mode method has good performance and practicality, so it shows great potential for environment testing in a simple and reliable way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Dual signal ratio
Detection of Au3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study on the performance of dual mode system for detecting Au3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation Project of Chongqing
Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstc2020jcyj-zdxmX0003) and Chongqing Entrepreneurship and Innovation Support Program for Returned Overseas Students (cx2022006).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of C-CDs preparation (A) and the working mechanism for the detection of Au3+ (B).

(A) TEM of C-CDs. The insets are HRTEM (bottom right) and histogram of the particle size distribution (upper right). (B) XRD pattern of C-CDs. (C) FTIR spectra of C-CDs before and after reaction with Au3+. (D) XPS survey scan. The high-resolution XPS spectra of (E) C 1s, (F) N 1s, and (G) O 1s of the C-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence excitation (EX) and emission spectra (EM) of C-CDs. Insets: photographs taken under visible light (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right). (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of C-CDs. Inset: Enlarge the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 320–420 nm.

(A) FL-SOS spectra of C-CDs and the corresponding photographs (inset in A) before and after the incubation of Au3+ for 0.5 h. Inset: C-CDs irradiated under UV light before (bottom left) and after (top left) reaction with Au3+, and C-CDs solution before and after reaction with Au3+ irradiated by green laser pointer (right). Concentrations of C-CDs and Au3+ are 5 % v/v and 100 μM, respectively. pH 6.0 (BR buffer). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of C-CDs before and after the incubation of Au3+ for 0.5 h and corresponding photographs (inset in B). Concentrations of C-CDs and Au3+ are 20 % v/v and 500 μM, respectively. pH 6.0 (BR buffer). (C) TEM of AuNPs reduced by C-CDs, magnified clusters, and (D) HRTEM (inset) images. (E) High-resolution XPS Au 4f spectra of AuNPs. (F) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs reduced by C-CDs, excitation and emission spectra of C-CDs. (G) Fluorescence lifetimes of C-CDs emitted at 490 nm before and after the incubation of Au3+ for 0.5 h. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A)The UV–vis absorption spectra of C-CDs after adding 0 to 700 μM Au3+ with diverse concentrations. (B) The linear relationship between the absorbance and the concentration of Au3+, with the corresponding images in visible light in the inset. (C) Fluorescence spectra of C-CDs after the addition of different concentrations of Au3+ (0–100 μM). (D) Two linear relationship between the value of S 770/F 490 and the concentration of Au3+. The inset shows photographs of C-CDs-Au3+ solution when irradiated at 365 nm. (E) Selectivity of C-CDs for Au3+ in colorimetric mode and corresponding inset (Conditions: Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe3+: 250 μM; Al3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ni2+: 2500 μM. Concentrations of C-CDs and Au3+ are 20 % v/v and 250 μM, respectively. BR buffer, pH 6.0. (F) Selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence pattern of C-CDs for Au3+ and the corresponding insets (conditions: Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe3+: 25 μM; Al3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ni2+: 250 μM. Concentrations of C-CDs and Au3+ are 5 % v/v and 25 μM, respectively. BR buffer, pH 6.0; λex = 385 nm).
Determination of Au3+ in Tap water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A dual-mode sensing platform coupling two-signal ratiometric and colorimetric methods for detecting Au3+ based on surface state-regulated carbon nanodot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The considerable risk posed by Au3+ residues to the environment and human health has sparked interest in researching Au3+ monitoring techniques. The detection results in the usual ratio mode are more reliable. In this work, we develop a dual-mode strategy based on reducing carbon dots coupling with two-signal ratiometric and colorimetric methods for high-sensitivity, good-selectivity, and wide-range detection of Au3+. Cyan carbon dots (C-CDs) were synthesized by a simple and efficient one-step hydrothermal method. The C-CDs with rich amino group used m-phenylenediamine as carbon source, which made it have the potential as a reducing agent. After the addition of Au3+, Au3+ was reduced to Au0, generating stable gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The fluorescence signal (F 490) of C-CDs decreased. At the same time, the large size of AuNPs enhances the second-order scattering signal (S 770) and produces the UV–visible absorption peak of AuNPs. Therefore, the dual-mode sensing strategy combining S 770/F 490 ratiometric and colorimetric detection of Au3+ is realized with high accuracy and sensitivity. Au3+ was determined in real samples and a good recovery was obtained. The dual-mode method has good performance and practicality, so it shows great potential for environment testing in a simple and reliable way.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold represents not only a precious metal, but also a source of nanomaterials that have garnered significant attention from the scientific community [1,2]. Gold-based nanomaterials exhibit interesting physicochemical properties and excellent biocompatibility [3,4]. For example, gold-based nanomaterials are often used for bioimaging, drug delivery, and the catalyst for various reactions. In addition, it has been evidenced by researchers that gold-based nanomaterials hold promise for photothermal therapy in various major diseases including rheumatoid arthritis, bronchial asthma, cancer, and malaria [5–7]. However, with the multifaceted development of gold-based nanomaterials in various fields, their widespread application inevitably leads to heavy metal pollution. Strong oxidants have reportedly been known to cause metallic gold to oxidize, producing gold cations (Au3+). The residual Au3+ left in inactive catalysts, insulating electronics, and other composite materials can somehow leach into soil and water [8]. Since Au3+ may bind strongly to biological molecules and seriously harm the liver, kidneys, and nervous system, it poses a substantial hazard to the environment and to human health [9,10]. The detection of Au3+ in the free state is therefore of practical importance. The detection of Au3+ and its reduction to Au0 is of great significance for environmental testing, human health, and the recycling of precious metal resources.

Compared to traditional methods of Au3+ detection, fluorescence analysis has gained favor among researchers due to its high sensitivity, low cost, and high specificity [11,12]. For example, Li et al. created hydrophilic red fluorescent carbon dots (referred to as CDs) by hydrothermally treating neutral red and thiourea to detect Au3+ with high sensitivity and selectivity because the redox reaction between sulfide groups on the surface of CDs and Au3+ enables the aggregation-induced quenching of CDs [13]. However, it is found that single signal is easily disturbed by environmental changes or background signals [14]. The ratiometric method can weaken the interference caused by environmental factors and provide a new idea for fluorescence detection due to its self-calibration characteristics [15–17]. An ideal fluorescence ratio sensor requires two signals at the same excitation wavelength to independently respond to the analyte. However, it is still a challenge to find fluorescent materials that fully meet the above requirements. For example, based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensors, the two fluorescence response signals are interrelated rather than independent [18–20]. Interference can easily lead to changes in the receptor signal while causing changes in the donor signal. Therefore, designing a new ratio-aware strategy is still an important subject. Unlike most constructed ratiometric platform with the same signal [21–23], our group previously reported that a new principle for developing ratiometric sensors by combining two kinds of signals: fluorescence (FL) and second-order scattering (SOS) [8]. The intensity of light scattering is highly dependent on the size and shape of the particles, and their variation offers the possibility of utilizing the light scattering signal. Second-order scattering (SOS) and fluorescence signals meet the requirements of simultaneous response at the same excitation wavelength. And these two signals are completely separate. They are two different and independent signals. The two different signals can be simultaneously obtained on a spectrofluorometer by rational design. Among the fluorescent materials that have been developed, CDs as a rising star of the carbon nanomaterial family exhibit typical fluorescence properties due to their quantum size effect [24–26]. Most importantly, the abundant functional groups on CDs surface give them good water solubility and potential oxidation or reduction ability [27]. Generally, the reductive species can reduce Au3+ to Au0 [28]. Au0 often exists in the form of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) or gold nanobelts, and the increase of its size may lead to the enhancement of SOS intensity [29]. Combined with fluorescent CDs, it is conducive to the construction of fluorescence scattering dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent probes for the accurate detection of Au3+. Therefore, the design of reducing fluorescent CDs is still a challenge.

In this work, a reducing carbon dots (C-CDs) based dual-mode platform coupled with FL/SOS ratiometric and colorimetric methods was proposed for the sensitive detection and reduction of Au3+. A schematic diagram of C-CDs preparation is illustrated in Fig. 1 A. The C-CDs were obtained by a one-step hydrothermal method using m-phenylenediamine as the carbon source. This process is simple and environmental-friendly. Moreover, the reducibility of C-CDs was originating from the surface-rich amino groups. After mixed with Au3+, the reducing C-CDs convert free Au3+ to AuNPs. So, the fluorescence signal (F 490) of C-CDs is diminished, while their SOS signal (S 770) is enhanced. This response is based on the synergistic effect of electron transfer and internal filtration between Au with different valence states and the C-CDs. The working mechanism for the detection of Au3+ is shown schematically in Fig. 1B. Furthermore, the C-CDs can detect Au3+ through a color change of the solution (from colorless to dark blue), allowing for a colorimetric method to quantify and identify Au3+. It was also applied to environmental water samples with satisfactory recovery rate. Compared to previous Au3+ sensing systems, our dual-mode detection method achieves a sensitive and practical way to detect Au3+. Importantly, since the method detects gold while converting the valence state of gold, it converts from highly toxic ions to gold-based nanomaterials with high application value, diminishing the impact on the environment. The successful construction of this sensing system is valuable for further development of functional CDs and provide a new approach in environmental detection and recycling of harmful metal ions.

Firstly, 0.01 g of m-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 1 min. Next, the solution was subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 16 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to eliminate the reaction residue and large particles. The filtered solution was dialyzed for 8 h (MW = 3500 Da), freeze-dried, and dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water for storage and further application.

The Au3+ quantitative dual-mode sensing system based on C-CDs consists of two methods: colorimetric method and S 770/F 490 ratiometric method. Firstly, 50 μL of C-CDs (5 mg mL−1) was diluted with 850 μL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH = 6, 0.02 M). Then, 100 μL of standard solution with varying concentrations of Au3+ was added to the system and allowed to incubate for 30 min at room temperature to record the absorption spectrum of the reaction solution. Secondly, 20 μL of 5 mg mL−1 C-CDs was diluted with 880 μL of BR buffer solution (pH = 6, 0.02 M). Then, 100 μL of standard solution with different concentrations of Au3+ was added to the system and incubated at room temperature for 30 min. After violent vibration, the fluorescence spectrum at excitation wavelength of 385 nm was measured. The fluorescence and the scattering intensities, F 490 and S 770, were recorded. And the ratio of S 770 to F 490 was used for quantitative analysis. The C-CDs was assessed based on testing other metal ions related to Au3+, including Hg2+, Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, and Ni2+. Each set of tests were carried out three times.

The actual water samples were analyzed experimentally. The tap water was taken from the laboratory, and the river water was taken from the Jialing river in Beibei District, Chongqing. The above water samples were centrifuged (8,000 rpm for 5 min) to remove insoluble particles, and then water samples treated with 0.22 μm filters were collected. 100 μL of standard Au3+ solution with different concentration gradients was added to tap water and river water, and incubated at room temperature for 30 min to prepare Au3+ spiked samples. Subsequently, 20 μL or 50 μL of a 5 mg mL−1 solution of C-CDs was mixed with spiked and unspiked samples, respectively. The samples were then diluted to 1 mL in BR buffer solution and incubated for 30 min at room temperature. After ensuring the homogeneity of the reaction solution by vigorous shaking, the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution were measured. The experiment was repeated three times under the same conditions.

In this study, the C-CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesis conditions of C-CDs were optimized in order to obtain the reduced C-CDs with excellent optical properties. As shown in Fig. S1, when the synthesis time was controlled at 8–20 h, the fluorescence intensity F 490 of C-CDs increased first and then decreased, and reached the best at 16 h. Meanwhile, Fig. S2 depicts that the F 490 of C-CDs increases between 160 °C and 200 °C and decreases between 200 °C and 220 °C. Therefore, 200 °C and 16 h were chosen as the optimal synthesis conditions.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were used to analyze the morphology and particle size distribution of the C-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the results show the C-CDs have good dispersion of spherical particles, with particle sizes ranging from 2 to 7 nm and an average size calculated to be 4.7 nm (as shown in the inset in the upper right corner of Fig. 2A). In addition, a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the lattice of graphitic carbon (100) plane [30], can be clearly observed in the high-resolution TEM image (HRTEM) (inset of Fig. 2A). The XRD pattern of the C-CDs shows a broad diffraction peak centered at 21.5°, corresponding to a carbon-based material with (002) lattice spacing, as shown in Fig. 2B. We also identify the surface groups of C-CDs in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2C). The results show that C-CDs contain three functional groups, CC/CN (1591/1514 cm−1), NH (3234 cm−1) and OH (3435 cm−1) [31,32]. The broad absorption peaks between 3435 and 3234 cm−1 confirm the abundant oxygen and nitrogen functional groups and their conjugates in C-CDs [33]. The chemical composition and surface functional groups of C-CDs were further investigated using XPS analysis. On the survey scan XPS pattern (Fig. 2D), C 1s (67.7 %), N 1s (14.23 %), and O 1s (18.07 %) were detected [34,35]. These bands were located at 284.8, 399.9, and 531.8 eV, respectively [36]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum shows CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN/CO (285.3 eV), and CO (288.4 eV) (Fig. 2E). The N 1s band has three peaks at 399, 401, and 402 eV, representing CNC, CNC, and amino-N, respectively (Fig. 2F). Meanwhile, the O 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2G) has two typical peaks at 531.8 eV (CO) and 532.4 eV (CO) [37]. The XPS results are in good agreement with the FTIR data. The above experimental results clarified that the C-CDs were successfully prepared.

The unique optical properties of CDs were determined by their easily modified surface structure [38,39]. Studying the optical properties of CDs is important for understanding their potential applications. In this study, we explored the optical properties of the synthesized CDs and investigated their behavior in aqueous solutions using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, we measured the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of C-CDs, and selected the excitation wavelength of 385 nm and the emission wavelength of 490 nm for further testing, respectively. There is a faint second-order scattering peak at 770 nm. In addition, Fig. 3B shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of C-CDs, and three absorption peaks of C-CDs appear at 220, 290 and 370 nm, respectively. The shoulder peak is located at 290 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition. The absorption peak at 370 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic conjugated system [40].

The above optical properties prompted us to construct a S 770/F 490 ratiometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensor for Au3+ detection. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the cyan fluorescence of C-CDs is highly sensitive to Au3+, with a sharp decrease in FL intensity at 490 nm after the addition of Au3+. In addition, there is a sharp increase in second-order scattering at 770 nm. It is known that changes in size and particles affect the scattering characteristics [41]. In this experiment, the transformation of different valence states of gold is accompanied by the change of its particle size, and the proportional sensing relationship between fluorescence and scattering is constructed. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 4B, the colorless aqueous solution of C-CDs almost has no absorption at 540 nm. After the addition of Au3+, the color of the solution changed from colorless to obvious purple-red (as shown in the inset of Fig. 4B), and a new UV–VIS absorption peak was generated at 540 nm. The S 770/F 490 ratiometric method is suitable for sensitive detection of Au3+ and the colorimetric method based on absorption change facilitates visualization of the phenomenon. The combination of the S 770/F 490 ratiometric and the colorimetric method can improve the reliability and sensitivity of experimental detection to some extent. This C-CDs-based dual-mode sensing system paves the way to develop the multifunctional CDs for environmental testing.

A series of experimental studies were carried out to understand the construction mechanism of Au3+ sensing platform. The results show that the formation of AuNPs is verified by TEM imaging (Fig. 4C). The TEM characterization enabled us to directly observe the shape and size of AuNPs. As shown in the HRTEM image in Fig. 4D, the typical lattice space is 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (111) lattice space of metallic gold. In addition, the peaks of 4f 7/2 (83.7 eV) and Au 4f5/2 (87.3 eV) indicate that the gold nanoparticles formed are in a metallic state [42,43] (Fig. 4E). It is noted that the characteristic plasma absorption peak of AuNPs overlaps well with the emission peak of C-CDs, and the fluorescence quenching principle is presumed to be related to internal filtering effect (IFE) (Fig. 4F). In addition, it has been studied that fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and IFE can be distinguished by measuring fluorescence lifetime [44]. Due to the energy transfer during FRET, the fluorescence lifetime is reduced, while the fluorescence lifetime during IFE is basically unchanged. Therefore, in Fig. 4G, the decay curves of C-CDs with and without Au3+ are measured. The fluorescence lifetimes of C-CDs are 1.27 and 1.28 ns, respectively, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of C-CDs is due to the IFE mechanism rather than the FRET mechanism. It can be seen from the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2C) and UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4B) that in the presence of Au3+, the amino content of C-CDs decreased and a new UV–vis absorption peak appeared. Au3+ is reduced to AuNPs, and the increase in its size results in an enhanced scattered signal. In summary, it is suggested that the mechanism of the sensing platform response of C-CDs is due to the synergistic effect of the electron transfer caused by amino-rich C-CDs and the IFE effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the performance of dual mode system for detecting Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection conditions were optimized in order to ensure optimum detection performance. Anions such as borates and phosphates with lone pair electrons are known to have significant metal binding properties. The binding of these anions to Au3+ regulates electrostatic interactions and attachment surfaces, which can accelerate the growth and aggregation of AuNPs [8]. The effect of pH value is shown in Fig. S3. At the same reaction time, the value of S 770/F 490 increases first and then decreases in the range from pH 4.0 to 8.0, and reaches the maximum at pH 6.0. In addition, in terms of optimal pH selection, the S 770/F 490 intensity value under BR buffer incubation is about 3 times that under NaOH/HCl conditions (Fig. S4A), indicating that the BR buffer is more sensitive to Au3+ reaction. As for the selection of BR buffer capacity, it can be seen from Fig. S4B that the concentration of buffer solution does not affect the growth and aggregation of AuNPs. In terms of reaction time, the value of S 770/F 490 tends to be stable and reaches the maximum value at 30 min (Fig. S5). Therefore, pH 6.0 and 30 min were chosen as the optimal conditions for the following experiments.

Under the optimized experimental conditions (BR buffer pH 6.0, 30 min), the dual-mode Au3+ detection system constructed with C-CDs was evaluated in detail. It can be seen from Fig. 5 A that as the concentration of Au3+ increases from 0 to 700 μM, the absorbance of C-CDs at 540 nm gradually increases, and the solution changes from colorless to purple-red (inset of Fig. 5B). Fig. 5B shows that there is a good linear relationship between the absorbance at the wavelength of 540 nm and Au3+ concentration (R2 = 0.990). The linear range is from 3 to 700 μM with the detection limit of 2.22 μM. Under the optimal conditions, Au3+ was quantitatively detected based on the ratiometric strategy. Fig. 5C shows that with the increase of Au3+ concentration, the cyan emission at 490 nm gradually decreased, while the SOS intensity at 770 nm gradually increased due to the formation of AuNPs. In the range of 0.06–10 μM and 10–100 μM, the F 490/S 770 ratio of the C-CDs sensing system is proportional to the concentration of Au3+, and the detection limit is as low as 35 nM (Fig. 5D). We further explore the response mechanism of two linear segments. C-CDs reduced Au3+ to AuNPs when detecting high concentrations of Au3+, and at the same time produced internal filtration between AuNPs and C-CDs. The mechanism is that the reduction reaction and internal filtration act synergistically. In contrast, when detecting low concentrations of Au3+, it can be seen from Fig. 5A that the absorption peak of C-CDs-Au3+ did not change much at low concentrations (<10 μM). We infer that in this case, the internal filtering effect is almost ineffective. Therefore, the F 490/S 770 ratio of C-CDs to Au3+ has two linear ranges of 0.06–10 μM and 10–100 μM. In summary, according to the mechanism exploration and linear relationship, it can be analyzed that the sensing platform response mechanism of C-CDs is the electron transfer caused by amino-rich C-CDs for low concentration of Au3+, and the synergistic effect of electron transfer and IFE caused by amino-rich C-CDs for high concentration of Au3+. Compared with other methods reported in previous studies (Table S1), the sensor proposed in this paper has a lower detection limit.

Moreover, the specificity of C-CDs for Au3+ was discussed for better application in practical assays. The effects of other metal ions on C-CDs were further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 5E and Fig. 5F, experiments were performed by colorimetric and S 770/F 490 ratiometric modes, respectively. The results showed that except for Hg2+, other metal ions had no significant effect on the Au3+ detection based on the reductive C-CDs. In order to achieve selective detection of Au3+, Cl− was used as a masking agent to mask Hg2+. It can be seen from Fig. S6 that 250 μM Cl− can basically eliminate the interference of Hg2+, and the effect on Au3+ is almost negligible. Therefore, the addition of the masking agent Cl− can mask Hg2+ and thus does not interfere with the detection of Au3+. It shows that C-CDs have potential application prospects in the detection of Au3+ in actual samples.

By analyzing the traditional ratio sensing platform (Table S1), most of the reported ratiometric sensing platforms are organic small molecule probes, which depend on the ratio of one signal. Compared with previous methods, the sensing platform used in this paper can collect multi-signal response from solo C-CDs for Au3+ detection, avoiding a more cumbersome construction process and improving the reliability of the results and the sensitivity of the method. In addition, it has some advantages for environment testing, such as high sensitivity, wide detection range, and no organic solvent.

Based on the high sensitivity and selectivity of C-CDs for Au3+, the practical application of the synthesized C-CDs was further evaluated. Tap water and Jialing river were used as real samples in this study. All samples were boiled for 3 min and centrifuged for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. After incubation with different concentrations of Au3+ for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity F 490, scattering intensity S 770, and UV–vis absorption values were detected. From Tables 1 and 2, the recoveries of the bimodal signals in tap water and Jialing river ranged from 99.32 % to 109.3 %, and the RSD obtained based on three replicate experiments were less than 4 %. These results indicate that the C-CDs have the potential to be a dual-mode probe for the detection of Au3+ in practical applications (Table 1 ).

In summary, a dual-mode probe for the detection of Au3+ by S 770/F 490 ratiometric and colorimetric methods was successfully constructed in this study. The C-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using m-phenylenediamine. The prepared C-CDs have amine and sp2 hybrid carbon, and have strong binding affinity for Au3+, which can effectively carry out electron transfer and reduce Au3+ to non-toxic AuNPs. The decrease of fluorescence intensity F 490, the enhancement of scattering intensity S 770, and the color change from colorless to dark blue after the addition of Au3+ in C-CDs are caused by electron transfer and IFE effect. The dual-mode platform has higher sensitivity and reliability with the comparison of single-mode detection. Therefore, this probe was successfully applied to the detection of Au3+ in real environmental water samples. The combination of S 770/F 490 ratiometric and colorimetric methods not only support sensitive detection, but also facilitate the visualization of practical applications. We hope this work paves the way for the development of environmental testing and multi-mode detection of substances.

Hong Yu Tan: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhe Sun: Data curation, Investigation. Cui Cui Deng: Conceptualization, Methodology. Bin Jie Wang: Investigation. Xue Zhen Dong: Methodology. Hong Qun Luo: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Nian Bing Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstc2020jcyj-zdxmX0003) and Chongqing Entrepreneurship and Innovation Support Program for Returned Overseas Students (cx2022006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.123993.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary material to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots embedded in mesoporous silica nanospheres: A simple platform for Cr(VI) detection in environmental water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Liu, T. Li, C. Zhang, Y. Zheng, C. Wu, J. Zhang, K. Zhang, Z. Zhang, Fluorescent carbon dots embedded in mesoporous silica nanospheres: A simple platform for Cr(VI) detection in environmental water, J. Hazard. Mater. 415 (2021), 125699. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.125699.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The golden age: gold nanoparticles for biomedicine
